title: Quiz: Exotic Options
description: Test your understanding of exotic option structures and pricing

questions.question: What makes an option 'path-dependent'?

options.text: It expires on a specific path date
Its payoff depends on the price history
It can only be traded at certain times

questions.explanation: Path-dependent options have payoffs that depend on the price path taken during the option's life, not just the final price at expiration. Examples include Asian, barrier, and lookback options.

questions.question: Why are Asian options typically cheaper than equivalent vanilla options?
questions.explanation: Asian options use averaged prices, which have lower volatility than spot prices (averaging smooths fluctuations). Since option value increases with volatility, lower effective volatility means lower option prices.

questions.question: What happens to a down-and-out barrier option if the barrier is touched?
questions.explanation: A down-and-out option is a knock-out barrier option that becomes worthless (is knocked out) if the underlying price falls to or below the barrier level.

questions.question: What does a floating strike lookback call allow the holder to do?

options.text: Sell at the maximum price during the option's life
Buy at the minimum price observed during the option's life

questions.explanation: A floating strike lookback call has a strike equal to the minimum price observed during the option's life. The payoff is (Final - Minimum), effectively letting the holder buy at the lowest price in hindsight.

questions.question: Lookback options are generally more expensive than vanilla options because they guarantee optimal timing in hindsight.
questions.explanation: True. Lookback options are more expensive because they guarantee the holder the best possible price during the option's life - essentially perfect market timing. This valuable feature commands a premium.

questions.question: What quantum advantage applies to Monte Carlo pricing of exotic options?
questions.explanation: Quantum amplitude estimation provides a quadratic speedup for Monte Carlo sampling - achieving error ε requires O(1/ε) quantum samples versus O(1/ε²) classical samples.

questions.question: What is the purpose of antithetic variates in Monte Carlo pricing?

options.text: To increase the number of simulations
To reduce variance by using negatively correlated pairs
To speed up random number generation

questions.explanation: Antithetic variates use pairs of paths (z and -z) that are negatively correlated. When averaged, this reduces variance compared to independent samples, improving convergence without additional computational cost.

questions.question: Which are path-dependent option types? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Asian (use average price), barrier (knock-in/out at levels), and lookback (use extreme prices) options are all path-dependent. European vanilla options depend only on the final price, not the path.
